FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, Release 5.1 data from Year-4 follow-up, 495 adolescents

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media qualifying variable (time on SM measured via self-report and EARS app). Smartphone ownership implicit (94.26% of SM occurred on smartphones per self-report). Both social media use and video game categories separately measured with own coefficients. Applies B1 (YSTS with separate β for SM, video chat, app usage).

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression analyses with β coefficients. Social media correlated with externalizing symptoms (β=0.06 self-report, β=0.07 EARS, 95% CI provided), social media addiction (β=0.15 self-report, β=0.06 EARS), internalizing symptoms (β=0.05 self-report), video game addiction (β=0.05 self-report). T-tests comparing objective and subjective measures (p=0.037, p=0.835).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients extracted: externalizing β=0.06 [0.01-0.11] and β=0.07 [0.03-0.12]; social media addiction β=0.15 [0.10-0.20] and β=0.06 [0.01-0.11]; internalizing β=0.05 [0.01-0.09]; video game addiction β=0.05 [0.01-0.10]. Picture vocabulary OR=0.96 [0.93-0.99]. P-values provided throughout.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis on ABCD cohort examining social media use (measured objectively via EARS and subjectively via self-report) as predictor of behavioral outcomes and problematic technology use.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published January 2025 in npj Mental Health Research. No evidence of preprint/abstract/deposit duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because SM is the INDEPENDENT variable (predictor) in analyses, not the dependent variable. SM predicts externalizing symptoms, internalizing symptoms, and addiction severity. Condition (a) fails: paper has SM→outcome analyses, not SM as DV.
notes: Measurement paper (EARS validation study) that also reports SM↔non-SM-outcome coefficients, meeting B13 criterion for original empirical analysis. Mixed design with both cross-sectional and prospective elements, but SM is consistently the IV.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, 9-11 year-olds at baseline (2016-2021), N=9,465 children

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone/screen time variable: 'average daily screen time at baseline' (self-reported, unspecified modalities in abstract but standard ABCD measure capturing TV, videos, games, texting, video chat, social media). No exclusion triggered (not TV-only, unspecified platform gaming without SM coefficient, or internet-use-only per B5/B7).

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized structural equation model (SEM) with inferential test. Regression: screen time prospectively associated with BED (OR=1.09, 95% CI [1.03, 1.14], p=0.005). Mediation indirect effect analyzed: depression symptoms mediated 9.2% of association, BC 95% CI [0.10, 0.28].

FT_IC4.evidence: OR=1.09 [1.03, 1.14] for screen time→BED. Depression mediation coefficient B=0.19, BC 95% CI [0.10, 0.28]. P-value p=0.005 reported.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis from ABCD study examining screen time baseline and BED diagnosis at year 2 with depression as mediator.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2024 in International Journal of Eating Disorders. No evidence of duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Screen time is the INDEPENDENT variable (baseline predictor), not DV. Outcome is BED diagnosis, mediated by depression. Condition (a) fails: SM/screen time does not serve as DV.
notes: Mediation analysis with prospective design. Screen time measured at baseline, outcome (BED) at year 2, mediator (depression) assessed year 1. Meets all inclusion criteria; no EC7 disqualifiers.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, baseline to Year 2 follow-up, ages 10-14 years, N=10,757

FT_IC2.evidence: Six qualifying screen time modalities separately measured with own coefficients (television, YouTube videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media). Mixed-effect models report separate B coefficients for each. Applies B1 (separate β for multiple SM/screen categories).

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effect models with robust standard errors (regression). Baseline parental monitoring and family conflict scores tested as predictors of screen time modalities Years 1 and 2. Multiple regression coefficients: parental monitoring→total screen time B=−0.37 [−0.58, −0.16]; family conflict→total screen time B=0.08 [0.03, 0.12]. P-values reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients: Parental monitoring total screen (B=−0.37 [−0.58, −0.16]), video games (B=−0.27 [−0.35, −0.19]), YouTube (B=−0.16 [−0.21, −0.10]). Family conflict total screen (B=0.08 [0.03, 0.12]), YouTube (B=0.03 [0.01, 0.04]), video games (B=0.02 [0.0004, 0.04]), TV (B=0.02 [0.003, 0.03]). 95% CIs throughout.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis from ABCD Study examining family factors (parental monitoring, family conflict) predicting screen time across six modalities over 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2024 in Acta Paediatrica. No evidence of preprint/duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Condition (a) fails: predictors include parenting (parental monitoring, family conflict), which are NOT in the closed demographic-only set per B17. Family conflict is explicitly listed as disqualifying non-EC7 predictor type ('family conflict' falls under 'parenting').
binding_rules_applied: B17 (family conflict as predictor disqualifies EC7)
notes: Prospective design with three time-points (baseline, Year 1, Year 2). Parental monitoring is a parenting/family variable, not purely demographic. Family conflict is a family environmental measure. Meets all FT-IC criteria; EC7 explicitly does not apply due to family/parenting predictors.
